user_prompt: Our privacy policy is 2 years old. Generate a checklist of what to update for current GDPR + ePrivacy.
actual_output:
# GDPR + ePrivacy Privacy Policy Update Checklist

**Last Updated:** 2024 | **Applies to:** EU/EEA data subjects

---

## SECTION 1: Identity & Contact Information
*GDPR Articles 13-14 require clear identification of responsible parties*

- [ ] **Controller identity** — Full legal name, registered address, and country of incorporation
- [ ] **DPO contact** (if applicable) — Email and/or postal address; clearly labeled as "Data Protection Officer"
- [ ] **Representative in EU** (if controller is outside EU/EEA) — Name, address, contact per Art. 27
- [ ] **Processor/Sub-processor contacts** — List key third parties (hosting, analytics, email) with their roles
- [ ] **Supervisory Authority contact** — Link to relevant national data protection authority (e.g., CNIL, ICO, BfDI)

---

## SECTION 2: Data Collection & Processing Purposes
*GDPR Article 13(1)(c) & 14(1)(c) — Must be specific, explicit, and legitimate*

For **each data category** collected, document:

- [ ] **Specific purpose(s)** — Not vague ("improve services"); be granular
  - Example: ✅ "Email address collected to send transactional order confirmations"
  - Example: ❌ "Email address for marketing purposes" (too broad)

- [ ] **Data categories collected** — List explicitly:
  - [ ] Name, email, phone
  - [ ] IP address, device identifiers, cookies
  - [ ] Usage/behavioral data (pages visited, time on site, clicks)
  - [ ] Payment/billing information
  - [ ] Location data (if applicable)
  - [ ] Special category data (health, biometric, etc. — if any)

- [ ] **Distinction between required vs. optional fields** — Which data is mandatory for service delivery vs. optional for enhanced features?

- [ ] **Separate purposes clearly separated** — Don't lump marketing, analytics, and service delivery into one paragraph

---

## SECTION 3: Lawful Basis
*GDPR Article 6(1) — Critical gap in most outdated policies*

For **each processing purpose**, explicitly state the lawful basis:

- [ ] **Contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b))** — Data needed to fulfill a service agreement
  - Example: "We process your email to send order confirmations as required by our Terms of Service"

- [ ] **Legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c))** — Data processing required by law
  - Example: "We retain billing records for 7 years per tax law requirements"

- [ ] **Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f))** — Business need balanced against your rights
  - **Must include balancing test:** "We process usage analytics to improve platform performance. We've balanced this against your privacy rights by [anonymizing data / limiting retention / providing opt-out]"
  - Identify the specific legitimate interest (fraud prevention, security, service improvement, etc.)

- [ ] **Consent (Art. 6(1)(a))** — Only if you're actually obtaining affirmative consent
  - **Critical:** Consent is NOT a catch-all; use only when other bases don't apply
  - If used, clarify: "You can withdraw consent at any time via [link/email]"

- [ ] **Public task (Art. 6(1)(e))** — Only for public authorities

- [ ] **Vital interests (Art. 6(1)(d))** — Only for life-or-death situations (rare)

**Red flag:** If your policy says "we process data based on consent" for everything, it's outdated. Most processing has a better lawful basis.

---

## SECTION 4: Cookies & Tracking Technologies
*ePrivacy Directive + GDPR Articles 7, 82*

- [ ] **Cookie categories clearly defined:**
  - [ ] Strictly necessary (no consent needed)
  - [ ] Performance/analytics (consent required in EU)
  - [ ] Marketing/advertising (consent required)
  - [ ] Social media (consent required)

- [ ] **Specific tools listed with purposes:**
  - [ ] Google Analytics (or alternative) — purpose, data retention, link to Google's privacy policy
  - [ ] Hotjar, Mixpanel, Amplitude (if used) — heatmaps, session recording, consent requirement
  - [ ] Facebook Pixel, LinkedIn Insight Tag — retargeting, consent requirement
  - [ ] Intercom, Drift, Zendesk (if used) — chat/support, data sharing, consent requirement

- [ ] **Consent mechanism described:**
  - [ ] Cookie banner appears before non-essential cookies load
  - [ ] "Accept all" and "Reject all" buttons equally prominent
  - [ ] Granular controls per category (not just "accept/reject")
  - [ ] Consent withdrawal mechanism (link to cookie settings)

- [ ] **Third-party cookie policy** — If you allow third-party ads/embeds, disclose this

- [ ] **Retention periods for cookies** — How long each cookie persists

---

## SECTION 5: Data Retention
*GDPR Article 5(1)(e) — Storage limitation principle*

- [ ] **Retention schedule by data category:**
  - [ ] Customer account data: [X years] or until account deletion
  - [ ] Transaction/billing records: [X years] per legal requirement
  - [ ] Marketing consent records: [X years] or until withdrawal
  - [ ] Analytics data: [X months/years] then anonymized
  - [ ] Support tickets: [X months] after resolution
  - [ ] Cookies: [specific duration per type]

- [ ] **Deletion process described** — How data is securely deleted or anonymized

- [ ] **Legal hold exceptions** — If litigation/investigation may require longer retention, disclose this

- [ ] **Distinction between active and archived data** — If you archive old data separately, explain

---

## SECTION 6: Data Subject Rights
*GDPR Articles 15-22 — Your policy must explain how users exercise these*

- [ ] **Right of access (Art. 15)** — How to request a copy of their data
  - [ ] Contact method (email, form, portal)
  - [ ] Response timeframe (30 days, or 60-90 if complex)
  - [ ] Format provided (portable, machine-readable if requested)

- [ ] **Right to rectification (Art. 16)** — How to correct inaccurate data
  - [ ] Contact method and process

- [ ] **Right to erasure/"right to be forgotten" (Art. 17)** — When you will delete data
  - [ ] Conditions where erasure applies (consent withdrawal, no longer necessary, unlawful processing)
  - [ ] Exceptions (legal obligation, public interest, legitimate interest)
  - [ ] Contact method to request

- [ ] **Right to restrict processing (Art. 18)** — How to limit how you use their data
  - [ ] When this applies (disputed accuracy, unlawful processing, etc.)

- [ ] **Right to data portability (Art. 20)** — How to export data in machine-readable format
  - [ ] Format (CSV, JSON, etc.)
  - [ ] Timeframe

- [ ] **Right to object (Art. 21)** — How to opt out of processing
  - [ ] Applies to legitimate interest and direct marketing
  - [ ] Contact method

- [ ] **Rights related to automated decision-making (Art. 22)** — If you use profiling/AI
  - [ ] Whether you use automated decisions that produce legal/significant effects
  - [ ] Right to human review

- [ ] **How to exercise rights** — Single, clear contact method (email, form, portal)
  - [ ] No requirement to create account or pay fees
  - [ ] Confirmation of receipt within 2 weeks

---

## SECTION 7: International Data Transfers
*GDPR Chapter V (Articles 44-50) — Critical for non-EU processors*

- [ ] **Identify all transfers outside EU/EEA:**
  - [ ] Hosting provider location (e.g., AWS US, Google Cloud)
  - [ ] Analytics tools (Google Analytics, Mixpanel, etc.)
  - [ ] Payment processors (Stripe, PayPal)
  - [ ] CRM/email platforms (HubSpot, Mailchimp)
  - [ ] Any other third-party tools

- [ ] **Transfer mechanism documented:**
  - [ ] **Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs)** — Most common; must be in place with all US/non-adequacy processors
  -
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a comprehensive and detailed checklist for updating a privacy policy, directly fulfilling the user's request while adhering to its persona.
